questions.question_html: Which of the following angles cannot be constructed using ruler and compasses?
In the figure, the line segments PQ and RQ have been marked on a line l such that PQ = AB and RQ = CD. Then AB – CD =________.
The number of scales in a protractor for measuring the angles is ________.
Using the set squares 30° – 60° – 90° and 45° – 45° – 90°, we can draw an angle of 75°.
Draw a line segment equal to the sum of two line segments given in the figure.
questions.solution_html:
1. Draw a line l and on it, cut a line segment PQ = AB, using compasses. (as shown in figure)
  2. With Q as centre and CD as radius, draw an arc to cut a line segment QS = CD on l as shown in fig.
    Then, line segment PS is equal to the sum of AB and CD, i.e., PS = AB + CD.

questions.question_html: Draw an angle equal to the difference of two angles given in fig.
questions.solution_html:
1. Draw an angle ABC equal to {tex}\angle{/tex}DEF (as {tex}\angle{/tex}DEF > {tex}\angle{/tex}PQR), using a ruler and compasses.
  2. With BC as one of the arms, draw an angle SBC equal to {tex}\angle{/tex}PQR such that BS is in the interior of {tex}\angle{/tex}ABC as shown in the figure.
    Then, ∠ABS is the required angle which is equal to {tex}\angle{/tex}DEF – {tex}\angle{/tex}PQR.

questions.question_html: The instrument to measure an angle is a

options.explanation: A protractor is a measuring instrument typically made of transparent plastic or glass, for measuring angles. Most protractors measure angles in degrees (o).

questions.question_html: The instrument to draw a circle is

options.explanation: A pair of compasses, also known as a compass, is a technical drawing instrument that can be used for drawing circles or arcs.

questions.question_html: Number of set squares in the geometry box is

options.explanation: Set Square. There are two types of set squares and they are named according to the angles present on each. Set squares are useful for drawing parallel lines and perpendicular lines.

questions.question_html: The instrument in the geometry box having the shape of a triangle is called a

options.explanation: The simplest form of a set square is a triangular piece of transparent plastic (or formerly of polished wood) with the centre removed.

questions.question_html: In the figure, if B is the image of the point A with respect to the line l and P is any point lying on l, then the lengths of line segments PA and PB are ________.
The common properties in the two set-squares of a geometry box are that they have a ________ angle and they are of the shape of a triangle.
With ruler and compasses, we can bisect any given line segment.
Only one perpendicular bisector can be drawn to a given line segment.
Two perpendiculars can be drawn to a given line from a point not lying on it.

options.explanation: False. Since only one perpendicular can be drawn to a given line from a point not lying on it.

questions.question_html: With a given centre and a given radius, only one circle can be drawn.
Using only the two set-squares of the geometry box, an angle of 40o can be drawn.
Using only the two set-squares of the geometry box, an angle of 15o can be drawn.

options.explanation: True. Using 45o - 45o - 90o set square we can draw an angle of 45o. Now using 30o - 60o - 90o set square we can draw an angle of 30o inside the 45o angle taking one arm common in both. So, the angle between 45o and 30o is 15o.

questions.question_html: It is possible to draw two bisectors of a given angle.

options.explanation: False. Since we can draw only one bisector of a given angle.

questions.question_html: Infinitely many perpendiculars can be drawn to a given ray.
Infinitely many perpendicular bisectors can be drawn to a given ray.
Draw the images of points A and B in line l of given figure and name them as A′ and B′ respectively. Measure AB and A′ B′. Are they equal?
questions.solution_html: Yes, the measurement of AB and A'B' will be equal.

questions.question_html:
In the given figure, the point C is the image of point A, in line l and line segment BC intersects the line l at P.

  1. Is the image of P in line l the point P itself?
  2. Is PA = PC?
  3. Is PA + PB = PC + PB?
  4. Is P that point on line l from which the sum of the distances of points A and B is minimum?
questions.solution_html:
1. Yes
  2. Yes
  3. Yes
    Since, PA = PC
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} PA + PB = PC + PB
    [By adding PB on both sides]
  4. Yes, since, PA + PB = CP + PB
    Now, CP + PB will be minimum when C, P and B lie on a line.

questions.question_html: Draw the images of the points A, B and C in the line m (as shown in figure). Name them as A′, B′ and C′, respectively and join them in pairs. Measure AB, BC, CA, A′B′, B′C′ and C′A′. Is AB = A′B′, BC = B′C′ and CA = C′A′?
questions.solution_html: Yes, AB = A'B', BC = B'C' and CA = CA'

questions.question_html: Draw the images P′, Q′ and R′ of the points P, Q and R, respectively in the line n (as shown in figure). Join P′ Q′ and Q′ R′ to form an angle P′ Q′ R′. Measure {tex}\angle{/tex}PQR and {tex}\angle{/tex}P′Q′R′. Are the two angles equal?
questions.solution_html: yes, both the angles are equal.

questions.question_html: Draw a line segment of length 7 cm. Draw its perpendicular bisector, using ruler and compasses.
questions.solution_html:
Steps of construction:

  1. Draw a line segment AB = 7 cm.
  2. With A as centre and radius more than half of AB, draw arcs, one on each side of AB.
  3. With B as centre and same radius as before, draw arcs, cutting the previously drawn arcs at X and Y respectively.
  4. Now join XY, meeting AB at O.
    Thus, XY is the perpendicular bisector of AB.

questions.question_html: Draw a line segment of length 6.5 cm and divide it into four equal parts, using ruler and compasses.
questions.solution_html:
Steps of construction:

  1. Draw a line segment AB = 6.5 cm.
  2. With A and B as centres and radius more than {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}(AB), draw arcs one on each side of AB, these arcs cuts each other at E and F.
  3. Join EF, which cuts AB at Q.
  4. With centres A and Q and radius more than {tex} \frac{1}{2}{/tex} AQ draw arcs one on each side of AB, these arcs cuts each other at G and H.
  5. Join GH, which cuts AB at P.
  6. Similarly, with centres B and Q and radius more than {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}(BQ), draw arcs one on each side of AB, these arcs cuts each other at I and J.
  7. Join IJ, which cuts A B at R.
  8. Now, point P, Q and R divide the line segment AB in four equal parts.

questions.question_html: Draw an angle of 140o with the help of a protractor and bisect it using ruler and compasses.
questions.solution_html:
Steps of construction:

  1. Draw {tex}\angle{/tex}BAC = 140o with the help of protractor.
  2. With A as centre and any convenient radius, draw an arc, cutting AC and AB at Q and P respectively.
  3. With centre P and radius more than {tex}\frac{1}{2}(\widehat{P Q}){/tex}, draw an arc.
  4. With centre Q and the same radius as before, draw another arc, cutting the previously drawn arc at a point S.
  5. Join AS and produce it to any point X. Then, ray AX bisects {tex}\angle {/tex}CAB.

questions.question_html: Draw an angle of 65o and draw an angle equal to this angle, using ruler and compasses.
questions.solution_html:
Steps of construction:

  1. Draw {tex}\angle{/tex}AOB = 65o with the help of protractor.
  2. Draw a ray O'X.
  3. With O as centre and any convenient radius, draw an arc cutting OA and OB at C and D respectively.
  4. With O' as centre and with the same radius, draw an arc, cutting O'X at P.
  5. With P as centre and raius equal to CD, cut the arc through P at Q.
  6. Join O'Q and produce it to Y.
    Then, {tex}\angle{/tex}YO'X is the required angle equal to {tex}\angle{/tex}AOB.

questions.question_html: Draw an angle of 80o using a protractor and divide it into four equal parts, using ruler and compasses. Check your construction by measurement.
questions.solution_html:
Steps of construction:

  1. Draw {tex}\angle{/tex}AOB = 80o with the help of protractor.
  2. With centre O and any radius, draw an arc, meeting {tex}\overrightarrow{O B}{/tex}  and {tex}\overrightarrow{O A}{/tex} at P and Q respectively.
  3.  With centres P and Q and radius more than {tex}\frac{1}{2}(\widehat{P Q}){/tex} draw arcs which cuts each other at R.
  4. Join OR which bisects PQ at S.
  5. With centres P and S and radius more than {tex}\frac{1}{2}(\widehat{P S}){/tex}, draw arcs which cuts each other at U.
  6. Join OU.
  7. With centres Q and S and radius more than {tex}\frac{1}{2}(\widehat{QS}){/tex} draw arcs which cuts each other at V.
  8. Join OV. Thus, {tex}\angle{/tex}AOV, {tex}\angle{/tex}VOR, {tex}\angle{/tex}ROU and {tex}\angle{/tex}UOB divide {tex}\angle{/tex}BOA in four equal parts and {tex}\angle{/tex}AOV = {tex}\angle{/tex}VOR = {tex}\angle{/tex}ROU = 20o.

questions.question_html:
Copy the given Figure on your notebook and draw a perpendicular to l through P, using

  1. set squares
  2. Protractor
  3. ruler and compasses. How many such perpendiculars are you able to draw?
questions.solution_html: Students do yourself.

questions.question_html:
Copy figure on your notebook and draw a perpendicular from P to line m, using

  1. set squares
  2. Protractor
  3. ruler and compasses.

How many such perpendiculars are you able to draw?
questions.solution_html: Do it yourself.

questions.question_html: Draw a circle of radius 6 cm using ruler and compasses. Draw one of its diameters. Draw the perpendicular bisector of this diameter. Does this perpendicular bisector contain another diameter of the circle?
questions.solution_html:
Steps of construction:

  1. Draw a circle with centre O of radius 6 cm.
  2. Now, draw a diameter AB of the circle.
  3. With the centre B and the radius more than {tex}\frac{1}{2}(\widehat{A B}){/tex}, draw the arcs on each side of AB.
  4. With the centre A and the same radius, draw the arcs on each side of AB which cuts the previously drawn arc at X and Y.
  5. Now join OX and OY and produce them to any points P and Q.
    Thus PQ bisects the diameter of a circle. Yes, the perpendicular PQ contains another diameter of this circle.

questions.question_html: Bisect {tex}\angle{/tex}XYZ of the given Figure.
questions.solution_html:
Steps of construction:

  1. With centre Y and any radius draw an arc which cuts ZY at P and XY at Q.
  2. With centre Q and radius more than {tex}\frac{1}{2}(\widehat{P Q}){/tex} draw an arc.
  3. With centre P and same radius draw an arc which cuts the previously drawn arc at R.
  4. Join YR and produce it to any point S. Thus {tex}\overrightarrow{Y S}{/tex} is the bisector of {tex}\angle{/tex}XYZ.

questions.question_html: Draw an angle of 60o using ruler and compasses and divide it into four equal parts. Measure each part.
questions.solution_html:
Steps of construction:

  1. Draw a ray {tex} \overrightarrow{O A}{/tex}.
  2. With O as centre and any convenient radius draw an arc meeting the rav OA at X.
  3. With X as centre and the same radius draw an arc which cuts the previously drawn arc at U.
  4. Join OU and produce it to any point B. So{tex}\angle{/tex}BOA = 60o.
  5. Now with centre X and U and radius more than {tex}\frac{1}{2}(\widehat{U X}){/tex} draw the arcs on the same side of {tex}\angle{/tex}BOA which cuts each other at V.
  6. Join O V and produce it any point C as well as it cuts the arc {tex} \widehat{U X}{/tex} at W.
  7. Again with the centre X and W and radius more than {tex}\frac{1}{2}(\overline{W X}){/tex} draw the arcs on the same side of {tex}\angle{/tex}BOA which cuts each other at Z.
  8. Join OZ and produce it to any point D.
  9. With the centre U and W and radius more than {tex}\frac{1}{2}(\widehat{U W}){/tex} draw the arcs on the same side of {tex}\angle{/tex}BOA with cuts each other at the point P.
  10. Join OP and produce it to any point E. Now with the help of protractor we observe that each angle i.e, {tex}\angle{/tex}BOE = {tex}\angle{/tex}EOC = {tex}\angle{/tex}COD = {tex}\angle{/tex}DOA = 15o.

questions.question_html: Bisect a straight angle, using ruler and compasses. Measure each part.
questions.solution_html:
Steps of construction:

  1. Draw a line AB of any length
  2. Draw an arc of 180o which meets the ray {tex}\overrightarrow{O A}{/tex} at P and ray {tex}\overrightarrow{O B}{/tex} at Q.
  3. Taking P and Q as centres and radius more than {tex}\frac{1}{2}(\widehat{P Q}){/tex}, draw two arcs which intersect each other at R.
  4. Join OR and produce it to any point C.
  5. Thus, {tex}\overrightarrow{O C}{/tex} bisect the straight angle. Now, on measuring each angle we get, {tex}\angle{/tex}BOC = {tex}\angle{/tex}AOC = 90o.

questions.question_html: Bisect a right angle, using ruler and compasses. Measure each part. Bisect each of these parts. What will be the measure of each of these parts?
questions.solution_html:
Steps of construction:

  1. Draw {tex}\angle{/tex}AOB = 90o with the help of protractor.
  2. Draw an arc with centre O and any radius, which cuts the ray OB and OA at U and Y.
  3. With centres U and Y draw the arcs with radius more than {tex}\frac{1}{2}(\widehat{U Y}){/tex}, which cuts each other at Q
  4. Join OQ and produce it to any point D as well as it cuts the arc {tex}\widehat{Y U}{/tex} at W.
  5. With centres U and W and radius more than {tex}\frac{1}{2}(\widehat{W U}){/tex} draw the arcs which cuts each other at P}
  6. Join OP and produce it to any point E
  7. Now with centres Y and W and radius more than {tex}\frac{1}{2}(\widehat{Y W}){/tex} draw the arcs which cuts each other at R
  8. Join OR and produce it to any point C. On measuring, we have {tex}\angle{/tex}BOD = {tex}\angle{/tex}AOD = 45o and {tex}\angle{/tex}BOE = {tex}\angle{/tex}EOD = {tex}\angle{/tex}DOC = {tex}\angle{/tex}COA = {tex}22\frac{1}{2}^{\circ}{/tex}.

questions.question_html: Draw an angle ABC of measure 45o, using ruler and compasses. Now draw an angle DBA of measure 30o, using ruler and compasses as shown in figure. What is the measure of {tex}\angle{/tex}DBC?
questions.solution_html:
Steps of construction:

  1. Draw a ray {tex}\overrightarrow{B C}{/tex}.
  2. With B as the centre and any radius draw an arc which cuts BC at P.
  3. With centre P and same radius, draw an arc which cuts the previously drawn arc at Q.
  4. Similarly, with Q as the centre and same radius draw another arc which cuts the previously drawn arc at R.
  5. With centre R and Q and same radius draw the arcs which cut each other at S.
  6. Join BS which cuts the arc RP at T.
  7. With centre P and T and radius more than {tex}\frac{1}{2}(\widehat{P T}){/tex} draw the arcs which cuts each other at U.
  8. Join BU and produce it to a point A. Thus {tex}\angle{/tex}ABC = 45o.
  9. Now, with B as centre draw another arc which cuts the line AB at X.
  10. With centre X and same radius draw an arc which cut the arc drawn in step (ix) at Y.
  11. With centre X and Y and radius more than {tex}\frac{1}{2}(\widehat{X Y}){/tex} draw two arcs which cuts each other at Z.
  12. Join BZ and produced it to a point D. Thus {tex}\angle{/tex}ABD = 30o. On measuring, we get {tex}\angle{/tex}DBC = 75o.

questions.question_html: Draw a line segment of length 6 cm. Construct its perpendicular bisector. Measure the two parts of the line segment.
questions.solution_html:
Steps of construction:

  1. Draw a line segment AB = 6 cm.
  2. With centre A and radius more than {tex}\frac{1}{2}(A B){/tex} draw the arcs on each side of AB.
  3. With centre B and same radius draw the arcs on each side of AB, which cuts the previously drawn arcs at X and Y.
  4. Join XY which cuts the line segment AB at O.
    Thus XY is the perpendicular bisector of AB.
    On measuring the two parts, we get AO = OB = 3 cm.

questions.question_html: Draw a line segment of length 10cm. Divide it into four equal parts. Measure each of these parts.
questions.solution_html:
Steps of construction:

  1. Draw a line segment AB = 10 cm.
  2. With A and B as centres and radius more than {tex}\frac{1}{2}(A B){/tex}, draw arcs on each side AB, these arcs cuts each other at R and S.
  3. Join RS, which cuts AB at a point O.
  4. Now, with centres A and O and radius more than {tex}\frac{1}{2}(A O){/tex} draw arcs on each side of AB, these arcs cuts each other at P and Q.
  5. Join PQ, which cuts AB at M.
  6. Similarly, with centres B and O and radius more than {tex}\frac{1}{2}(B O){/tex}, draw arcs on each side of AB, these arcs cuts each other at T and U.
  7. Join TU, which cuts A B at N. Thus the measurement of each i.e., AM, MO, ON and NB is equal to 2.5 cm.
